generationRationale: Shifted from a definition of a natural magnet to an application distinguishing man-made magnets.
text: While lodestone occurs naturally in the earth, which of the following is an example of an artificial, man-made magnet?
explanation: Lodestone and magnetite are natural magnets found in nature. A bar magnet is manufactured by humans using magnetic materials.

generationRationale: Converted a TRUE_FALSE about magnetic strength into a practical scenario testing where paperclips would stick.
text: If you roll a bar magnet in a pile of iron paperclips, where will the majority of the paperclips stick?
options: Evenly distributed across the entire magnet
explanation: The magnetic strength of a bar magnet is strongest at its poles (the ends) and weakest in the middle. Therefore, most paperclips will cluster at the ends.

generationRationale: Shifted to testing the reason why a suspended magnet aligns north-south.
text: A freely suspended bar magnet aligns itself in the north-south direction because of the gravitational pull of the Earth.
explanation: It aligns in the north-south direction because the Earth itself acts like a giant magnet, not because of gravity. The magnet aligns with Earth's magnetic field.

generationRationale: Tested the geographical vs magnetic pole concept in a fill-in-the-blanks format.
text: The pole of a freely suspended magnet that points towards the geographic south of the Earth is called the ________ pole.
explanation: Just as the north-seeking end is the north pole, the south-seeking end that points toward geographic south is the south pole.
